title: Free DIY Opal Cutting Machine Guide
excerpt: A complete guide to building your own DIY opal cutting machine on a budget.
content:
Cutting opal on a knife block or piece of sandpaper by hand is a worthwhile learning experience but is also very time consuming and hard on the hands. So, if you're on a budget and looking to upgrade, doing your own DIY Opal Cutting machine can be a great next step for your learning experience.

Now using commercial machinery is not something I would recommend bleeding your account dry for unless you're a professional jeweller wanting to expand to a commercial enterprise. If you're crafty like me and so many others, than you can find a way to make your own DIY Opal Cutting machine and for a fraction of the price and let me tell you, there's nothing quite like the satisfaction of creating a set up of your own.

## Getting the parts together for your DIY Opal Cutting Machine

This is an AC motor and it is a quality vintage motor. Not everyone will be able to get this type of motor, but I highly advise finding a motor like this one and this can be done by calling up scrap yards or contacting older family members that may have old machinery lying around in the back shed.

This motor in particular is an old industrial laundry motor of all things. The most important thing to note when sourcing a motor is the RPMs. The ideal motor will need to be 1400 rpm max but if you can find a 1600 rpm motor it can be slowed using a pulley system.

Next, you'll need a shaft or arbor that fits the measurement of your motor. They can be found very affordably on eBay or Amazon. At this point you have one of two options, you can either follow my first design by welding a circular plate measuring 150mm onto the shaft of the motor or if you have a higher RPM machine you will need to use a pulley system with an arbor for sanding wheels.

Following my initial design, once the plate is welded on to the shaft, the hard part is over and you can exhale slowly. Most of the following steps can be completed with a quick trip to Bunnings.

To soften the surface of the plate I used 150mm wide protective foam. If you can get adhesive foam that's even better but if you cannot, you'll need to buy spray on glue, I use Selley's Kwik Grip. You'll also need 1200 grit sandpaper and 600 grit sandpaper.

Once the foam is dry you can then apply the grit of sandpaper you prefer to the padding which you'll need to cut to size also using kwik grip if needed. The great thing about this is, once the grit wears, it's easily removable.

Next you'll need some piping and a small plastic tap to insert into the piping. How you do your water set up and splash guard is your choice.

title: Opal Cutting: A Free Beginner's Guide
excerpt: Opal is mysterious and wonderful. I don't think the uniqueness of opal is matched by many other things in this world.
content:
If you've selected this blog, it's because you have a genuine interest in opal cutting and want to hone your skills. Opal is mysterious and wonderful. I don't think the uniqueness of opal is matched by many other things in this world and as such, understanding and cutting opal can be difficult even for experienced opal cutters at times.

When you're first getting into Opal cutting, it can be very difficult and you can be making mistakes you're not even aware of - trust me, I know - so the most important thing above all else is to start slow and Practice, Practice, Practice!

But the great thing about dipping your toes into opal is that a beginner Opal cutting set up is very budget-friendly and easily accessible from any hardware store.

## Opal Cutting: Before You Begin

On the top of this opal there is a black layer of potch, in the middle there are a few colour bars intersecting each other and depending on which bar is better will determine how the opal is cut. On the bottom of the opal, there is a layer of sand.

Potch is colourless opal and has the same hardness as the colour bar, so practicing with potch first is always a great way to dipping your toes into opal cutting.

## What You'll Need

- Knife block OR 600 grit Sandpaper
- 1200 grit Sandpaper
- Spray bottle filled with water
- Towel and a stable surface to work on
- Optional: cerium oxide solution or powder
- Optional: Piece of leather or Dremel and polishing buff attachment

## Opal Cutting Steps

1. Place your knife block on a flat surface and wet the block with water.
2. Start rubbing the edges of your opal to give you a better idea of the shape.
3. Once you have an approximate shape, begin with an oval shape as this is an easier starting point.
4. Begin to work the surface of the opal by getting rid of any unnecessary parent material.
5. Create a good base on your opal leaving enough thickness while getting rid of rough material.
6. Once the base is smooth, create a setting edge by rounding out sharp edges.
7. Focus on the colour top. Don't rub the face flat on the surface.
8. Switch to 1200 grit sandpaper to create a smooth surface.
9. The final stage is polishing with cerium oxide.

If you're having trouble or would like further advice, feel free to reach out. We all gotta start somewhere.

title: Free Jewellery Hack: How to Figure Out Your Ring Size
excerpt: Are you having trouble figuring out your ring size? We've noted some quick and easy jewellery hacks for helping you figure this out!
content:
Are you having trouble figuring out your ring size? The Good Opal Co. are here to help.

The Good Opal Co. is committed to helping our customers and providing the best customer service experience we can. That's why once a week we write blogs like this, to help you, our customers by providing you with helpful information to make informed purchases.

## Method 1: Using an Existing Ring (Diameter)

Diameter is the measurement across the inner width of a ring, going from one side to the other.

**You'll need:**
- Tape measure or ruler
- A ring that fits you

**Step 1:** Make sure the ring you're using fits the finger you plan to wear it on.

**Step 2:** Using your ruler or tape measure, measure the widest points inside the ring from edge to edge.

## Method 2: Using String (Circumference)

Circumference is the measurement around the base of the finger.

**You'll need:**
- String or strip of paper
- Pen or marker
- Tape measure or ruler

**Step 1:** Wrap a string comfortably around the desired finger.

**Step 2:** Mark the spot where the string or paper meets and feels comfortable.

**Step 3:** Lay the string on a flat surface and measure its length in millimetres.

## Helpful Sizing Tips

**When choosing your size:** Always choose the size of the largest finger on which you'll be wearing the ring.

**Stackables:** For a more comfortable fit, consider going one size up from your normal size.

**Wide Shank:** If the ring has a wider shank, we recommend going one size up from your normal size.

title: Indigenous Origins in Opal
excerpt: Exploring the rich dreamtime stories and Indigenous Australian connection to opal spanning over 60,000 years.
content:
I would like to start this blog by acknowledging the Traditional Owners of the land on which I live, the Wurundjeri People. I pay my respects to their Elders, past and present, and the Aboriginal Elders of other communities.

Indigenous Australia has a rich aural history spanning over 60,000 years before the appearance of European settlers that extends to dreamtime about opal long before its 'official' discovery in Listowel Downs near Adavale in Queensland in 1869.

## South Australian Opal Dreamtime

For the Aboriginal countries around Andamooka in South Australia, it is believed that opal was created when the Indigenous Australian's ancestral being was conveyed to earth via a great rainbow. Where the rainbow connected with the land, great rocks and pebbles formed and glittered in the sun with all the colours of the rainbow.

## NSW Opal Dreamtime

My favourite dreamtime story is told by the Yuwaaliyaay people of Wallangulla (Lightning Ridge) country. The supreme spirit Bhiamie and his two wives were being stalked by Gurria the Great Crocodile. Gurria swallowed the wives whole but Bhiamie tracked and speared him. As Gurria died, it rained, and a rainbow appeared. The colours of the rainbow were trapped in Gurria's scales, and with it, opal was formed in the ground.

## Queensland Opal Dreamtime

A dreaming from Central Queensland tells the story of the 'weeping opal' from a time when a giant opal ruled over the land. This Aboriginal ancestor came to being after two tribes had been warring. One warrior threw a boulder so hard that it became stuck in the sky, expanded, and burst revealing the colours of a huge opal.

title: How to Value Opal
excerpt: Opal is the most difficult of all gemstones to value. Learn the key factors that determine an opal's worth.
content:
So, you want to learn how to value Opal? You've come to the right place! Opal is the most difficult of all gemstones to value. Why? Because each opal is a unique one of a kind piece, each pattern and colouring different in its own way.

## Body Tone

Body tone is one of the most important factors in the valuation of opals. Body tone refers to the background or the 'underlying colour' of the opal, which ranges from black through dark to light. The darker an opal the more valuable, because a stone with a darker body tone tends to display colours more vibrantly.

Black opal is the most valued opal and can reach prices of up to AUD $40,000 per gram.

- Black opals have a body tone of N1 to N4
- Opals with N5 to N6 are considered semi black or dark opal
- Body tone N7 to N9 are called crystal opals

## Play of Colour

The play-of-colour is the range of colours displayed in the opal. The most common colour in opal is purple or blue and the rarest colour is Red (fire). Red colouring is caused by larger microscopic silica spheres, whereas blue is caused by the more common small spheres.

## Direction of Colour

Opals dramatically change appearance based on viewing angle. Opals that face with strong fire from all directions make good ring stones. An opal that faces in one direction is ideal for a pendant.

## Pattern

The pattern is unique to every individual opal. Excellent patterns include:
- Harlequin - large sections of colour like a mosaic
- Flagstone - large sections of colour with straight edges
- Ribbon - narrow, parallel cascading lines of rolling colour
- Rolling Flash - large sections of colour which roll across the stone
- Pinfire - tiny points or specks of colour

## Shape

Oval cut opals are generally considered more valuable than free form. A cabochon stone is more valuable when it has a high dome compared to a flat stone.

title: Is Opal a Good Investment?
excerpt: For years people have been investing in gold and silver. But why invest in opal? Learn about opal as a safe investment.
content:
So, you'd like to learn about investing in opal? For years people have been investing in alloys and metals such as gold and silver. So, why invest in opal?

Because unlike silver and gold, the value of opal does not depend on stocks and fluctuate in such a manner. Opal is a rare commodity that cannot be industrialised due to the nature of how it is sourced. Australian opal is highly prized, rare and the best quality opal in the world.

Opal is considered a safe investment because the material has never dipped in value and has only ever increased due to the demand of the world market and jewellery production.

## Why Black Opal is a good investment

The most valuable Black opals from the Lightning Ridge area with signature fiery red colour is widely considered by most financial advisors to be the best opal investment. These are incredibly rare varieties of opals with incredible rare patterns which are highly desirable.

## A bit of background

When you purchase opals for investments, they should always come with a certificate of authenticity. The Opal industry has seen this firsthand with the closure of Mintabie and Lambina mines, whereby the value of opals from these areas has skyrocketed due to scarcity.

## Investing in Opal Doublets

Whilst solid opals are the best investment, the popularity of doublets has increased. Doublets provide people with an affordable source of Australian opal that can look just as good at a fraction of the cost.

## Opal Investment Pro Tips

- Anything between N1 and N4 is considered black opal - always a sound investment
- N5 and N6 are dark opals, N7 and N8 are light opals
- When buying opal, the source of the material is important
- Avoid opals with inclusions or a poor cut
- Oval or circle cut high domed cabochon opals are most desirable

title: Lightning Ridge Opal
excerpt: At The Good Opal co. our favourite opal material to cut is Lightning Ridge Opal. Here's everything you need to know.
content:
At The Good Opal co. our favourite opal material to cut is Lightning Ridge Opal. Lightning Ridge Opal cuts incredibly generously compared to opal from other parts of Australia. It can be more predictable which is an opal cutter's dream.

## Lightning Ridge Opal Types

### Crystal Opal

Unlike other forms of opal, in crystal opal the colour can run all the way through the entire stone. The flash of colour in crystal opals is often incredibly vibrant and bright, even more so at times than Black Opal. Purple colouring is the most common type of Crystal Opal. Red is the rarest crystal opal colour.

### Black Opal

Black opal is the rarest and most valuable gem in the world. Top of the range gem quality black opal can be worth up to AUD $40,000 per gram. Black opal bares its name due to the dark body tone - the colour of the opal when you turn it over. Lightning Ridge is the black opal capital of the world, producing 95% of the world's Black Opal.

### White Opal

White opals are the most common form of opal found. There is a fine line between white opal and crystal opal. When a white opal is partially translucent, it often enhances the clarity and vibrancy of the colour.

### Fossil Opal

Opal forms in cavities within rocks. If the cavity was once a living thing - a bone or shell - the opal can form a fossil replica. Lightning Ridge exclusively produces Land animal fossils including Dinosaur Opal fossils of species never before discovered.

## Lightning Ridge Opal Fields

- The Three Mile - The most productive area on the field
- Coocoran - Produces an estimated 80% of the black opal output
- Grawin - Very extensive fields with large amount of opal won
- Sheepyard - Found in 1985, centre of a major opal rush

title: How Opal is Mined and Cut
excerpt: Learn about the fascinating process of how opal is mined and how to tell if an opal will cut well.
content:
## How Opal is Mined

When the first opal mine shafts in Australia were being dug, they didn't have the diesel equipment used today. In the early days, shaft sinking with a pick, shovel and nothing but a candle for light was the way of the Opal Trade.

A shaft is sunk straight down until promising opal dirt is discovered. Once found, the miner would tunnel down to the opal level and then branch out sideways, following the level of opal.

For seam opal, miners follow veins of potch found in the walls of the mine in hopes that the veins will turn to colour. Both shaft and open-cut mining takes place at Lightning Ridge.

In Queensland, the majority of mines are large open-cut operations. Boulder material is removed from the ground using an excavator.

## How We Source Opal

At The Good Opal co. we are committed to sourcing all opal ethically and directly from Australian Miners. A majority of the material we source is from Lightning Ridge and Coober Pedy.

## How to Tell if an Opal Will Cut

There are a few tricks to know if an opal will or won't cut:

- Shine a torch through it to see colour bars and imperfections
- Check if the colour bar runs all the way around the opal
- The thickness of the colour bar is the most promising sign

## Opal Cutting Tips

1. Get a small torch and hold the opal to the light
2. Start with Potch to hone your skills
3. When you start to cut, always rub the edges first
4. Less is more when it comes to opal rubbing
5. The base of the opal is usually the side with thickest potch
6. Don't rub the face of the opal flat

title: Learn How to Cut Opal: Cabbing 101
excerpt: Important knowledge you should be aware of before you start cabbing opals on a machine.
content:
In previous blogs we've helped our customers learn how to cut opal without a cabbing machine. In this blog we are going to provide some important knowledge you should be aware of before you start cabbing opals.

## Opal Formation

When opal was formed through crevices in the earth filled with silica rich water 100 million years ago, silica deposits were left behind. These deposits were spherical, and it is their stacking that creates gaps between the spheres. When light passes through the spheres and their gaps, it splits like a rainbow from a prism.

Rough opal often has three layers: potch, host rock (sandstone), and the colour bar. The aim of cutting opal is to remove the sand or host rock, use the potch to create a base, and cut the opal to display the colour bar in the best possible way.

## Cabbing 101

The quickest and easiest way to cut opal is using a cabbing machine because of:
- The speed of the machine
- The size of the wheels covers more area
- Greater accuracy since you can use both hands

## Learn How To Cut Opal The Right Way

To operate a cabbing machine, you'll need both hard wheels and soft wheels.

**Hard wheels** are diamond sintered wheels used to quickly get through rough material, potch, and host rock. Take your time on hard wheels. Start on higher grits if you are unsure.

**Soft wheels** differ from hard wheels in everything from manufacture to operation. Soft wheels are when you can start to apply more pressure. They are the most important step in creating a polished opal.

## Key Tips

- When applying the opal to the dop stick, ensure it is level
- Plan what shape you would like before you start cutting
- Draw the shape you would like onto the opal as a cutting guide
- Make sure your lighting is bright enough
- Check the opal as you go

title: Top 10 Tips for Buying Opal Jewellery
excerpt: Buying opal jewellery online can be tricky. We've outlined things to consider before you buy.
content:
## Buying Opal Jewellery: Handmade vs Mass Produced

Buying Opal jewellery online can be tricky. There's a big difference between handmade and mass produced jewellery. Whilst mass produced jewellery has consistency, it's often not produced using quality or ethical materials and will often not last a lifetime.

Handmade jewellery is unique, made with care, and made to last using ethical materials by creators paid a living wage.

## Our Top 10 Tips to Buying Opal Jewellery Online

1. Shop small - your opal jewellery is much more likely to be made with real opal and quality care

2. If you don't see what you like, talk to the creator - they'll most likely be happy to help

3. Solid opal is best - doublets and triplets are great budget options but solid opals last longest

4. If buying an opal to set in jewellery, be sure it's real and not lab made

5. If it doesn't say whether the opal is solid or not, ask. You don't want to pay for solid and receive a doublet

6. If the opal appears like a window looking into an opal or looks magnified, it's most likely a triplet

7. If the piece has an open back and the black backing is shiny or glass-like, it's likely a doublet or triplet

8. 925 doesn't always mean real silver - buy from trusted stores, preferably Australian businesses

9. Check your ring size before you buy. If unsure, contact the maker for help

10. Use a ring sizer or our ring sizing guide to ensure proper fit

title: Mintabie Opal
excerpt: Mintabie opal is arguably some of the most unique opal in Australia, producing white, crystal and black opal.
content:
Mintabie opal is arguably some of the most unique opal in Australia. The mines of Mintabie produce white, crystal and the illustrious black opal but the layering of colour bars is unique, making picture stones that are often sliced against rather than along.

The town of Mintabie is in the Anangu Pitjantjatjara Yankunytjatjara in South Australia, leased to the Government of South Australia for opal mining purposes since the 1980s.

## Mintabie Opal History

Indigenous Australians were the first people to find opals in Mintabie; during the First World War, they traded in black opals at Coober Pedy. Conditions were harsh, and it was only in 1976, with the help of large new machinery, that non-Indigenous people came to the area.

Mintabie's harsh climate and lack of water discouraged mining until mid-1970. In October 1981, the Pitjantjatjara Land Rights Act came into force and Mintabie became part of a large area of freehold Aboriginal land.

In February 2018 the state government declared that it would not renew any leases in the town. With the closure, no miner could maintain residency or renew opal mining claims.

## Mintabie Opal Geology and Mining

The geology of this specific opal is quite different from other areas in Australia. These crystal opals are typically found in the Lower Cretaceous bed. The host rock is Ordovician, much older than Cretaceous rock, making it difficult to mine. Modern miners tend to utilize heavy bulldozers or explosives.

## Cutting Mintabie Opal

Mintabie opal is stronger than lightning ridge material which is great for preserving the opal, but it does make for more difficult cutting. When colour bars are mashed together, they can be near impossible to cut a colour rich stone, but they can cut great picture stones.

title: Coober Pedy Opal
excerpt: Coober Pedy is the White Opal Capital of the world, producing over 80% of the world's supply of White opals.
content:
Coober Pedy Opal was first discovered in 1915, when a man was prospecting for gold and came upon the white opal material. Coober Pedy (derived from the Aboriginal words 'Kupa Piti' meaning 'white man in a hole') produces the most white opal.

At the Good Opal co, our favourite type of white opal is Coober Pedy white opal fossils-shells. Due to the semi translucent nature of shells, once fossilised the vibrancy of their colour is breathtaking.

## Coober Pedy White Opal

Coober Pedy is the White Opal Capital of the world, producing over 80% of the world's supply of White opals. White Opals are distinguished by their pale white or light body tone, and most commonly their gorgeous play of colour, red and pink in particular.

White opals often have white potch left on the back of the stone. When a white opal is partially translucent, it often enhances the clarity and vibrancy of the colour.

Funnily enough, where with black opal red is the rarest and most valuable colour, on white opal red occurs much more often. Good quality white opal may fetch prices up to AUD $1000 per gram.

## Coober Pedy Crystal Opal

Coober Pedy also famously produces vibrant crystal opal and exclusively marine fossil opals due to the positioning of Coober Pedy where the great ancient basin once was.

Crystal opals are unique because the colour can run all the way through the entire stone. The flash of colour is often incredibly vibrant and bright.

## Coober Pedy Opal Formation

Opal forms in cavities within rocks. Coober Pedy produces fossils of saltwater or marine organisms, due to the positioning of the ancient Eromanga Sea 100 million years ago, and has produced opal fossil specimens worth up to 1 million dollars.

title: Andamooka Opal
excerpt: Andamooka is one of the oldest known opal fields in Australia, with opals famously used in the Queen's jewellery.
content:
Andamooka is one of the oldest known opal fields in Australia, with opals from this region famously having been used in the Queen's jewellery. Unlike any other Australian opal, variations of Andamooka Opal can be treated with sugar or acid to turn it black and improve the opal play of colour.

The word Andamooka is derived from an Aboriginal name, meaning 'Large Waterhole'. Fun Fact: Andamooka is the only Australian town where none of the streets are named.

## Andamooka Opal Fields

In 1872 a pastoral lease was taken out for Andamooka Station. In 1930 two boundary riders found opal at the site now known as Treloar's Hill. The story goes they were caught in a thunderstorm and whilst sheltering under a tree a coloured rock caught their eye.

Unlike other Opal Fields in Australia, the Andamooka Fields contain significant variations of host rock including quartzite, shale, sandstone, and limestone. These variations make incredible difference to how the opal is formed.

This includes Matrix Opal (often darkened to enhance colour), crystal opal, dark opal, light opal, white opal, concrete opal or 'painted ladies' as well as fossil opal.

## Treating Matrix Opal

The equipment used to treat Matrix opal is very basic and can even be done at home:

1. Place a mixture of 1 cup of sugar in a Pyrex bowl with equivalent water and heat until dissolved
2. The stone needs to be cut, shaped and sanded but not polished before treating
3. Place stones in the sugar syrup solution and cook for 8 hours plus
4. After cooking let them cool down
5. Polish with leather lap with 50,000 grade diamond paste

Treated opal is not valued as highly as naturally dark opal. The treatment only penetrates through a very thin layer of material.

title: Queensland Boulder Opal
excerpt: Boulder opals are only found in Queensland, Australia, easily distinguished by their layer of solid brown ironstone.
content:
Boulder opals are only found in Queensland, Australia. Primary opal fields include Winton, Opalton, Quilpie, Yowah, Eromanga, Cunnamulla, and Longreach.

They are easily distinguished by their layer of solid brown ironstone left on the back of the stone. As the name suggests, this variation of opal is mined from large ironstone boulders under the ground, often via open cut mining using an excavator.

The stones are more commonly cut in freeform shape to best showcase the veins of colour. The ironstone backing is what makes the opal naturally vivid, creating what is often referred to as a natural doublet.

Due to the significant weight difference in iron stone vs sandstone, valuing this type of opal per carat is not common practice. Pieces free of ironstone can fetch up to $15,000 USD per gram.

## Unique Boulder Opal Patterns

- **Koroit opal/Yowah nut:** precious opal formed in siliceous ironstone nodules and ripples of colour
- **Opal Matrix:** opal formed in the infillings of pores or holes between grains of the host rock

## Formation

Boulder Opal is unique to the eastern shore of the ancient inland sea with vibrant colour found in seams which can be cleaved apart. Rather than silica rich water becoming trapped in faults, the opal forms within the concretion of the ironstone itself, commonly forming thin veins of colour.

## Boulder Opal Cutting Tips

Cutting Boulder Opal is a little different to cutting other forms of Australian Opal. It is much messier to cut (beware, it stains everything).

Most boulder opal is cut with a flat face because the colour is often very thin. The best approach is following the veins of colour in the iron stone. When polishing, use 50,000 grit diamond polishing compound as opposed to cerium oxide, which can fill voids leaving white dots.

title: What to Do in Lightning Ridge
excerpt: Lightning Ridge is home to black opal mining but there's so much more which brings tourists to this remote part of NSW.
content:
Lightning Ridge is home to the world's largest black opal mining which is the reason it's become a well known town in Australia. But there's so much more which brings tourists to this remote part of NSW. Think hot springs, a unique underground sculpture gallery, a beer can house and so much more!

The Car Door Tours are free self guided tours in Lightning Ridge. You'll have green, yellow, red and blue car door tours to choose from. The Green Door tour is a must if you like sunsets and strange landscapes.

## Driving from Melbourne to Lightning Ridge

The trip is at least 13 hours without stops. Best advice:

- Snacks and lots of water
- Get petrol whenever you see a station
- Sunglasses and sunscreen is a must
- Download your maps in case signal drops
- Know how to change a tyre
- Book accommodation well in advance
- A travel buddy is always a great idea
- Allow half a day's rest when you arrive
- Buy a 24 pack of water - the bore water tastes like it
- Bring bug repellent! A fly net wouldn't be the worst idea

## Site Seeing Musts

**Amigo's Castle** - This amazing hand-built castle was a labour of love from 1981 to 2001. One of the most famous attractions in Lightning Ridge.

**Artesian Bore Baths** - A trip to the Ridge would not be complete without a visit to the free baths. Open all the time except 10am-12noon weekdays for cleaning.

**Australian Opal Centre** - Meet a life-like dinosaur face to face! Examine rare opalised fossils.

**Chambers of the Black Hand** - Amazing underground sculptures including an Egyptian chamber and the Last Supper.

**Fossicking Heap** - Nothing more exciting than that first bit of colour appearing beneath your fingertips.

## Good Eats

Bruno's Italian Restaurant for great pizza. Piccolo's for 3 Cheese pasta and Oysters Mornay! The Bowls Club makes a mean parmi and is air conditioned.

title: Top 10 Opal Jewellery Making Tips
excerpt: Setting opals can be tricky, especially when you're used to setting gemstones with different hardness.
content:
Setting opals can be tricky, especially when you're used to setting gemstones with different hardness - opals being the softest. But there are some great tricks for preventing damage to your opals when setting as well as repairing unfortunate mistakes.

## Opal Jewellery Making Tips

**Tip 1:** Apply a layer of masking or fabric tape to the face of your opal to protect it from nicks or scratches when using pliers or bezel/prong pushers.

**Tip 2:** Place a piece of floss beneath your opal when bezel setting, so that if you need to remove or reposition the opal, you can without damaging it.

**Tip 3:** Place your finger over the opal when bezel pushing so that any slips of the tool will hit your finger as opposed to smashing the opal.

**Tip 4:** Calculate the circumference of your opal to get a precise measurement for the bezel wire for a tight fit.

**Tip 5:** If your opal is too big for the setting, DO NOT FORCE IT unless you want it to crack. Settings with too much tension can cause cracks at the slightest knock.

**Tip 6:** Low prong settings look sleek but ensure you create a secure enough seat for the opal or the low prongs won't hold long term.

**Tip 7:** If there's a noticeable gap between the opal and bezel edge, tape the opal's face and gently file the edge, then use a burnisher to smooth it down.

**Tip 8:** If your anxiety is getting the better of you and things aren't going well - stop. Return tomorrow with fresh eyes.

**Tip 9:** If you chip or scratch your opal, wrap 1200 grit wet and dry sandpaper around a Dremel attachment. Keep the opal wet throughout, then finish by polishing with cerium oxide.

**Tip 10:** Piece looks dull? Use Cerium oxide - the polish of the opal gods. It transforms stones and works for all metals too.

title: DIY Jewellery Hack: Cleaning Your Jewels at Home
excerpt: There's a common misconception that if silver jewellery tarnishes it's ruined. Here's how to clean it at home!
content:
There's a common misconception that if silver jewellery tarnishes (turns brown or dull in colour) it's not genuine silver or has 'gone bad' and is ruined. This being the reason many people prefer gold to silver as gold does not tarnish.

Silver tarnish is natural and is simply the process of the silver reacting to exposure to oxygen and is completely reversible. That's why it's often advised to keep jewellery in a box when not being worn.

## Cleaning Opal Jewellery Hack 1

It's important to remember not to clean your opal jewellery with any type of abrasive as this will damage the surface of your opal.

**First:** Wash your opal jewellery in soapy water with a soft tooth brush focusing more on the silver metal than the opal itself. If you're unhappy with the opal's lustre, it can be repolished by an opal cutter.

**Second:** Follow up by polishing the ring with a polish cloth in circular motions - this you can do on the opal as well without fear of damaging it.

## Cleaning Jewellery (non opal) Hack 2

If your jewellery is more severely tarnished, do a bi carb soda scrub first:

1. Get a small bowl and make a mix of 2-3 teaspoons of bi carb soda with enough warm water to cover
2. Drop the jewellery in the mix, stir and leave it for a couple of minutes
3. Scrub with a toothbrush
4. Wash in dish soap
5. Finish with a hand polish using a polish cloth

You can use the old can of coke trick but it takes longer and isn't as effective.

Remember: no matter how much wear and tear your jewellery has seen, any competent jeweller can repair the scratches and bring your jewellery back to brand new condition.
